title: Thoughts on Iruels Intentions
wikitext:
[]: Thoughts on Iruel's Intentions             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue Dec 27, 2011 5:19 am We all know the story, so let's just jump in with some important notes: - Initially, Iruel was occupying a relatively small area of a protein wall. It was not until the test with the simulation Eva bodies that it decided to act. Specifically, it was not until Rei connected remotely to Unit-00 and expanded its AT-Field. The first target of infection for Iruel was Rei's simulation body. - After the invasion had begun, Gendo quickly ordered that the Evangelions be evacuated from the facilities, with special emphasis on Unit-01. He explains himself by asserting that the Evangelions are useless if they become infected. He also notes that the attack is too close to Adam. - Upon breaching the Prinbow Box, Iruel used the computers inside to hack the rest of headquarters, specifically the Magi, and attempted to initiate a self-destruct sequence. It appears to me that using the Magi was not originally in Iruel's plan. Using the supercomputers to self-destruct everything? It would be achieving a large handful of nothing by blowing everything up, including itself. Destruction for the sake of destruction is too simple for an entity that had been waiting patiently all this time. The fact that it waited until Rei had expanded Unit-00's AT-Field with the simulation body is important. It managed to infect most of the simulation body, and planned to use it, as demonstrated by how it reached its hand out. Iruel wanted Eva, but for what? Had Iruel merged with Unit-01, could it have caused Third Impact? The rules for Third Impact are a little vague, as there are many versions, but the fusion of Angel and what is essentially Lilith seems to qualify. This would explains Gendo's priority for Unit-01's evacuation (assuming he needs more reason other than "KEEP MY YUI SAFE"). Had Iruel used any Evangelion (no matter which), could it have used the Eva's body to reach Adam or Lilith? Gendo seems concerned with how close this invasion is to Adam, and at this point in the series, all we know is that Adam had made it to Nerv in an embryonic state; and no mention of Lilith is ever made until much later. Confusion is produced here, simply because of Kaji's mislabeling of the white giant in Terminal Dogma in a later episode (some believe it's simply bad writing). Either way, the plot of this episode will have us believe that the Angel coming into contact with Adam concerns Gendo. However, Iruel failed to make it to any of the Evangelions. It probably quickly realized it simply infected a simulation body, and not the real thing, but now it had been exposed and had to fight for its life. So, it quickly goes for the next best thing: the Magi. And the rest is history. It got backed into a corner, and decided to blow everything up. And then got tricked into terminating itself. End of story. Iruel, despite successfully infiltrating Nerv and almost blowing the place up, is a pretty incompetent Angel.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Tue Dec 27, 2011 2:28 pm Obligatory "Angels seem to think Evangelions are Adam" point.

[]:             Quote Post by rhfxz1s » Tue Dec 27, 2011 4:14 pm Can Iruel actually "fuse" with its targets or merely control them? I guess it might not really matter since the exact definition of what a Third Impact-causing fusion is kind of vague, but it seems like it's possible that simply getting control of a an Eva/Adam/Lilith/etc and causing Third Impact might be different things.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue Dec 27, 2011 5:53 pm rhfxz1s wrote: Can Iruel actually "fuse" with its targets or merely control them? I guess it might not really matter since the exact definition of what a Third Impact-causing fusion is kind of vague, but it seems like it's possible that simply getting control of a an Eva/Adam/Lilith/etc and causing Third Impact might be different things.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Tue Dec 27, 2011 6:01 pm Doesn't an Eva need its own S2 engine to cause TI? Seele didn't seem to be afraid Gendo would use Unit 1 for that purpose until it had a functioning S2.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Tue Dec 27, 2011 6:19 pm Chuckman wrote: Doesn't an Eva need its own S2 engine to cause TI? Seele didn't seem to be afraid Gendo would use Unit 1 for that purpose until it had a functioning S2.

[]:             Quote Post by Ceimoa Nan » Wed Dec 28, 2011 2:37 pm When first watching the series, I assumed some Angels intended to only destroy NERV HQ, without trying to reach Adam or Lilith, somehow aware that it would make another Angel's job (of reaching Adam or Lilith) a lot easier. In other words, if Tokyo-3 and NERV HQ become a giant crater, there's nothing stopping the next Angel from just walking in and initiating Third Impact. Sahaquiel and Iruel are the only ones I can think of that I'm pretty certain planned to destroy themselves. Then again, maybe their AT Fields would've kept them alive, even after colliding with Earth or using Magi to blow up NERV HQ, and they would've crawled through the wreckage towards Adam or Lilith in a weakened form. And my theory would mean that at least some of the Angels were aware of each other's existence, and working together. If Iruel intended to destroy NERV HQ from the start, then it would've infected the simulation body simply as a substrate with which to hack Magi. So, basically, if the Angels know that destroying NERV HQ will make the next Angel's chances of initiating Third Impact easier, or if Iruel could've survived NERV HQ's destruction, then Iruel wouldn't just be "achieving a large handful of nothing." But it would also make sense that Iruel originally planned to infect an Evangelion and use it to reach Adam or Lilith, or maybe just have its microorganisms spread and reach Adam or Lilith without using an Eva, and only tried hacking Magi once it was quarantined off from its targets. In this case, the simulation bodies would just be a launching platform, something to make a large number of Iruel microorganisms out of to spread through NERV HQ, or something to make a computer out of. Question: I always thought that Iruel had basically turned the simulation body into a computer. Once the simulation body was infected, Iruel rearranged its constituents into something like circuitry, and then hacked the other NERV computers and Magi remotely. Was that what happened, or did Iruel infect the actual computers in the Pribnow Box? The ones in the observation deck that got flooded?

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Wed Dec 28, 2011 4:45 pm Episode 24 lets us know that a self-destruction of Nerv HQ would also take out Terminal Dogma and Lilith with it. Had Iruel succeeded in this, everything would be destroyed, and any means of initiating Third Impact would be nixed, thus achieving a handful of nothing. I also don't by into the theory that the Angels are somehow making things easier for the next one, or even aware of each other on any sort of cognitive level. Ceimoa Nan wrote: Was that what happened, or did Iruel infect the actual computers in the Pribnow Box? The ones in the observation deck that got flooded?

[]:             Quote Post by Monk Ed » Wed Dec 28, 2011 9:06 pm Sachi wrote: Prinbow Box

[]:             Quote Post by Sharaz Destler » Thu Dec 29, 2011 3:25 pm Haha, funny. What I got from it was (and Sachi may or may not have refuted my theory), the Angels get a little more "creative" with every defeat. Because a succession of big, stompy monsters failed to do the job, the next one was a tiny microorganism. Sort of an adaptation to circumstances...if someone wants to add their own take on my idea, they're welcome to do so.

[]:             Quote Post by Lorkhan » Thu Dec 29, 2011 11:55 pm Sharaz Destler wrote: Haha, funny. What I got from it was (and Sachi may or may not have refuted my theory), the Angels get a little more "creative" with every defeat. Because a succession of big, stompy monsters failed to do the job, the next one was a tiny microorganism. Sort of an adaptation to circumstances...if someone wants to add their own take on my idea, they're welcome to do so.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Fri Dec 30, 2011 12:35 am There isn't really a pattern to how the Angels decide to act. Ramiel was a very patient Angel, yet was among the first. Zeruel was a very aggressive Angel, yet was among the last. If any pattern exists at all, it's that the Angels are introduced in a manner that best serves the plot and character development, but that doesn't really count, now does it?

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Fri Dec 30, 2011 10:38 am There is a vague pattern. Shamshel is focused on the tactic that worked best for Sachiel (melee), when that fails, Ramiel is the complete opposite (a powerful ranged attack), Gaghiel is both adapted to the specific environment where it attacks and an attempt at an improved defense through sheer mass (presumably, if on land, it would have been so huge that damaging it would have been profoundly difficult). Israfel is a response to the failure of singular angels to achieve their objective, and (I presume) Sandalphon would have avoided the issue entirely by digging through the ground. Iruel is both tiny and able to adapt on the fly where big, slowly adapting angels failed, and Sahaquiel is an all out attack. Leliel is an all out defense, to the point where it really has no offensive capability and just sits there. Zeruel is the final offensive variant, able to both outlast and outgun everything it gets hit with. When the Evas defeat the absolute best the angels can offer offensively, they start getting really weird, and try to merge with the Evas or the pilots themselves. The only ones that don't fit are Tabris, being artificially created (although, a humanoid "stealth" angel is a likely iteration) and Matariel, because Matariel is the worst angel. I mean, it cries at you. That's it. Bardiel is odd because it shows up too early. The attempt to take over an Eva should have happened after Zeruel in order to fit a logical progression, but there may be an explanation. The storm cloud may have been a larval form of the Angel and it partially merged with the Eva by accident or something, but I'm going into pure fan wank territory now.

[]:             Quote Post by Lorkhan » Fri Dec 30, 2011 12:19 pm Bardiel is odd because it shows up too early. The attempt to take over an Eva should have happened after Zeruel in order to fit a logical progression, but there may be an explanation.

[]: Re: Thoughts on Iruel's Intentions             Quote Post by Bagheera » Sun Jan 01, 2012 1:23 pm Sachi wrote: And then got tricked into terminating itself. End of story. Iruel, despite successfully infiltrating Nerv and almost blowing the place up, is a pretty incompetent Angel.

[]:             Quote Post by Radjenovic » Sun Jan 01, 2012 2:45 pm I beleived that the Angel had tried infecting all three test bodies as all the pilots make comments about feeling odd, and Asuka said "My right arm feels clear but the rest of my senses are fuzzy" I allways beleived it was this particular angels plan to initiate contact with the pilots as the final 3 angels tried to make contact or even take over the bodies and minds of the pilots, The 11th angel only becomes active when the AT field is put up so if it were a self eveolving being the AT field would be a threat and it reacted to that, still seeking a host it went after the next biological brain it could invade, after the pilots were ejected the only organic brain it could infect were located within the magi(When Dr. Akagi opens the case inside the Magi it looks like a brain to me)

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun Jan 01, 2012 7:50 pm Radjenovic wrote: I beleived that the Angel had tried infecting all three test bodies as all the pilots make comments about feeling odd, and Asuka said "My right arm feels clear but the rest of my senses are fuzzy"

[]:             Quote Post by Ceimoa Nan » Sun Jan 01, 2012 9:01 pm Sachi wrote: Episode 24 lets us know that a self-destruction of Nerv HQ would also take out Terminal Dogma and Lilith with it.

[]:             Quote Post by Damaijin » Sun Jan 01, 2012 9:47 pm I'm not entirely sure Iruel "waited". The tech guys were talking to Fuyutsuki about shoddy workmanship in different areas of the compound due to time and money constraints. That's probably how Iruel wound up inside Nerv unnoticed, and then made its way through the base like mold behind walls. I won't deny that the test might have influenced its behavior, but I think it was just happenstance, not causality. Also, at that point, I don't think Iruel was physically large enough to support a brain capable of "planning", nor am I convinced that it ever developed one at all. As for its reason for infecting the test-bodies, I'm thinking it was pretty much "they were just there". As it multiplied and traveled through the walls, it had effects on the materials it was occupying, so it seems only natural that once it made contact with the test-bodies, it was able to affect them as well. The reason it reached for the control-booth window is probably because Rei's test body only had its right arm. Since the control-booth window was directly to its right, any movement of the arm at all would only bring it closer to the glass. As for why it tried to blow the Geofront to hell, the fact is that the action was quite brilliant in a stupid way. Consider its behavior up until that point: Whenever Iruel met with a threat/obstacle in its environment, it instinctively took direct action to overcome said obstacle in order to survive and propagate. It corroded the compound walls to facilitate its spread; it developed a way to use O3 as an energy source rather than being poisoned by it; and it's worth noting that it didn't even produce its AT Field until it was apparent that it couldn't survive the lasers. As it adapted and continued to spread, it eventually made contact with the computers, became a part of them (like with the test-body), and then began to spread further through the network systems. When Nerv tech experts tried to shut it down and lock it in/out over the wires, Iruel did exactly what it had been doing from the start: survive. It was being attacked technologically, so it naturally sought to remove the technological threat. In this instance, making the computer destroy itself was probably the most direct and efficient method to take it out. The fact that it would have destroyed itself in the process probably didn't even occur to it. (Hence the "brilliant in a stupid way".) Other concrete details that disprove Iruel's intelligence (and thus motives) include: 1. Ritsuko's plan to stop Iruel was literally tricking it into saving itself, which meant it wasn't aware of the repercussions of the self-detonation. 2. Iruel remained inactive inside the Magi system for the rest of the series. This puts the kibosh on self-awareness, let alone intentions or agenda. (If an angel knew it was an angel, why would it work with humans as integrated soft/hardware, even if it meant survival?) 3. It was specifically stated four episodes later that Leliel was the first of the Angels to possess what could be considered intelligence. Also, Gendo called Iruel the embodiment of evolution. Evolution doesn't think, it just happens.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun Jan 01, 2012 10:39 pm Damaijin wrote: The tech guys were talking to Fuyutsuki about shoddy workmanship in different areas of the compound due to time and money constraints. That's probably how Iruel wound up inside Nerv unnoticed, and then made its way through the base like mold behind walls.

It was being attacked technologically, so it naturally sought to remove the technological threat. In this instance, making the computer destroy itself was probably the most direct and efficient method to take it out. The fact that it would have destroyed itself in the process probably didn't even occur to it. (Hence the "brilliant in a stupid way".) Other concrete details that disprove Iruel's intelligence (and thus motives) include: 1. Ritsuko's plan to stop Iruel was literally tricking it into saving itself, which meant it wasn't aware of the repercussions of the self-detonation. 2. Iruel remained inactive inside the Magi system for the rest of the series. This puts the kibosh on self-awareness, let alone intentions or agenda. (If an angel knew it was an angel, why would it work with humans as integrated soft/hardware, even if it meant survival?) 3. It was specifically stated four episodes later that Leliel was the first of the Angels to possess what could be considered intelligence. Also, Gendo called Iruel the embodiment of evolution. Evolution doesn't think, it just happens.

[]:             Quote Post by Sachi » Sun Jan 01, 2012 10:39 pm Damaijin wrote: The tech guys were talking to Fuyutsuki about shoddy workmanship in different areas of the compound due to time and money constraints. That's probably how Iruel wound up inside Nerv unnoticed, and then made its way through the base like mold behind walls.
